frameworks.name: Bayesian heading estimation model
frameworks.one_line: perceptual estimate computed by combining likelihood with a prior inferred from discrimination data

frameworks.name: efficient sensory encoding
frameworks.one_line: heading direction encoded according to a prior derived from human discrimination performance

frameworks.name: linear perception-action mapping
frameworks.one_line: perceptual estimate linearly scaled to response by a range-dependent factor

core_question: Why do reported biases in heading estimation from optic flow vary in direction across studies?
key_assumptions: the sensory encoding of heading is efficient given a prior inferred from discrimination data
perceptual estimate is linearly scaled into a response by a scaling factor
the scaling factor depends monotonically on the size of the response range
manipulations: size of the response range allowed to participants
three psychophysical experiments comparing reporting conditions
key_findings: heading estimates monotonically scale with the size of the response range
small response ranges yield underestimation (central bias)
large response ranges yield overestimation (peripheral bias)
the proposed Bayesian model quantitatively accounts for participants' reports
interpretations: conflicting bias reports reflect response-range effects, not differences in underlying heading perception
a linear perception-action mapping reconciles central vs peripheral bias findings
